title: LLMs, Cheap Answers, and the Economics of Questions

# LLMs, Cheap Answers, and the Economics of Questions

Large language models are the latest in a long sequence of technologies — from search engines to recommendation algorithms to generative AI — that have driven down the cost of producing answers. What distinguishes LLMs is not just the speed or volume of their output but its character: the answers they produce are immediate, fluent, and above all plausible. Plausibility turns out to be a more dangerous property than outright wrongness. A clearly wrong answer invites correction; a plausible one invites acceptance. When an answer feels good enough, the inquiry stops.

### The plausibility trap

In attention-scarce environments saturated with content, plausibility functions as a proxy for truth. Search results that confirm existing assumptions float to the top. Memes that reinforce priors spread furthest. Language models, trained to produce text that sounds authoritative and coherent, complete thoughts in ways that feel right without necessarily reducing what remains unknown. The better these systems become at sounding correct, the easier it becomes to mistake fluency for insight — and the harder it becomes to justify the cognitive expense of pushing further.

For audiences conditioned by infinite scrolling and algorithmic feeds, the pull of cheap, abundant answers is strong. Continuing to ask — especially when the next question might destabilize a comfortable conclusion — carries a real cost in effort and attention. The economics are asymmetric: answers are getting cheaper while good questions are getting more expensive.

### Information theory and the value of answers

Shannon's information theory offers a precise framework for thinking about this asymmetry. Information is valuable in proportion to the uncertainty it resolves. A weather forecast confirming sunshine during a drought tells you nothing; a forecast of unexpected rain changes your behavior. By this measure, not all answers are equal. An answer that sounds authoritative but merely restates what was already assumed — or what could have been trivially generated — carries little informational value regardless of how polished it appears.

LLMs are prolific generators of low-entropy answers: text that is coherent and confident but that often adds little to what the questioner already believed or could have guessed. The sheer volume compounds the problem. In knowledge-rich, attention-poor environments, the bottleneck is not access to facts but allocation of attention. Systems optimized for speed and verbosity — designed to answer rather than to surface ambiguity or incompleteness — consume the scarce resource of attention without proportionally reducing uncertainty. A genuinely valuable answer is one that resolves meaningful uncertainty while respecting the limits of the attention it demands.

The deeper trap is the assumption that more answers are better than fewer. Accumulating plausible responses to poorly framed questions does not converge on understanding. It produces the sensation of knowledge without its substance — a kind of informational junk food that satisfies without nourishing.

### Why questions matter more in unstable environments

The value of good questions increases as the environment becomes less predictable. In stable systems with well-understood rules, answers retain their utility over time. But the contemporary landscape is marked by structural uncertainty — situations where the rules themselves shift, where historical patterns break down, and where static knowledge has limited shelf life. In such conditions, the capacity to stay curious, to sustain a line of inquiry, and to reframe problems becomes more important than any particular stock of answers.

The historical record bears this out. Copernicus did not produce a better answer to the question of how epicycles work; he asked whether the Earth was actually at the center. Einstein did not refine Newtonian mechanics; he questioned the assumption that time and space were absolute. CRISPR sat in the scientific literature for two decades as a description of a bacterial immune mechanism before someone reframed it as a potential gene-editing tool. In each case, the breakthrough was not an answer to an existing question but a question that exposed the limitations of the prevailing framework. The value was generated not by resolving uncertainty within an established paradigm but by restructuring the paradigm itself.

These examples share a common structure: a question that bridges domains or levels of abstraction, connecting ideas that did not previously belong to the same conversation. Shannon's own contribution followed this pattern — he did not build a better amplifier but asked what information fundamentally was, and the reframing generated compounding value across telecommunications, biology, cryptography, and physics. Questions of this kind expand the field of awareness rather than narrowing it toward a single answer. They are generative rather than convergent.

### The human-plus-LLM asymmetry

Here a surprising dynamic emerges. LLMs, for all their limitations as autonomous thinkers, are remarkably good at connecting unconnected domains — surfacing analogies, drawing on patterns across vast corpora, and juxtaposing ideas that a single human mind would be unlikely to hold simultaneously. But this capacity is activated only by the right prompt, which is to say, by the right question.

In the hands of someone seeking a quick, cheap answer, an LLM is a liability. It will produce something plausible, the user will accept it, and the inquiry will end prematurely. In the hands of someone asking a genuinely good question — one that probes ambiguity, bridges distant domains, or challenges an assumption — the same LLM becomes a powerful instrument for exploration. The technology amplifies whatever orientation the user brings to it. It makes lazy thinkers lazier and curious thinkers more capable.

This asymmetry has significant implications. Structural uncertainty — the kind that characterizes the most consequential problems — is often resolved at unexpected intersections between distant ideas. Only a question can bridge concepts that do not already belong to the same system. An LLM can traverse those bridges at speed, but it cannot decide which bridges are worth building. The human contribution is not to generate more text but to identify which directions of inquiry are worth pursuing, to recognize when a plausible answer is masking a deeper confusion, and to resist the gravitational pull of easy closure.

The economics, then, are clear. As the cost of generating answers approaches zero, the scarce resource is no longer information but the judgment to know which information matters, the discipline to keep asking when a comfortable answer is already available, and the imagination to frame questions that open new territory. Technologies that make answers cheap make questions valuable — and the practitioners who thrive will be those who understand that the most important thing an LLM can help you do is not answer faster but ask better.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-017|Information Theory and the Power of Reframing Questions]] — grounds the Shannon framework applied here in its full historical context, demonstrating how a single reframing question generated compounding value across domains and decades
- [[z20260315-016|The Impact of Photography on Art and Its Parallel to AI]] — exemplifies the same structural pattern in a different domain, where a technology commoditized a previously scarce output and the most successful response was to redefine what mattered rather than compete on the old terms
- [[z20260315-010|The Left's Intellectual Disengagement from AI and Its Political Consequences]] — contrasts with the orientation argued here, illustrating how treating LLMs as fundamentally unserious forecloses precisely the kind of questioning that would be most productive
- [[z20260315-008|The Transformation of the Software Engineering Role in the Age of AI Coding Agents]] — applies the questions-over-answers dynamic to a concrete professional domain, where the engineer's value migrates from producing code to planning, judgment, and problem selection as AI commoditizes implementation
- [[z20260315-011|Historical Skepticism Toward New Technologies]] — situates the cheap-answers dynamic within the broader recurring pattern of technologies restructuring where value resides, with adoption proceeding regardless of resistance once sufficient utility is demonstrated
